content:
IT

InstaTunnel Team

Published by our engineering team

Rootless Edge Deployments: Architecting Daemonless CI/CD Pipelines with Podman and Buildah

Quick answer

Bypassing docker.sock: Daemonless Pipeline Orchestration : webhook testing answer

For local webhook testing, run your app locally, expose it with a public HTTPS tunnel, and paste the stable callback URL into the provider dashboard.

How do I test webhooks on localhost?

Start your local server, open a public HTTPS tunnel to that port, configure the provider webhook URL, and inspect events in your local logs.

Why does a stable webhook URL matter?

Stable URLs prevent provider dashboards from needing manual callback updates every time you restart a tunnel.

Exposing a root-level Docker socket in a pipeline that has direct access to your local physical hardware is a supply-chain time bomb. It’s time to sever the daemon completely. Here’s how to architect fully rootless, daemonless deployment pipelines using Podman and Buildah — and what’s actually changed in the rootless container ecosystem through mid-2026.

• The Vulnerability of the Shared Socket
In traditional CI/CD environments, reliance on Docker has established a pervasive architectural anti-pattern: mounting the root-level Docker daemon socket (/var/run/docker.sock) directly inside pipeline runners. Often called Docker-in-Docker (DinD) or socket-mounting, this pattern grants the runner process full administrative access to the host engine. It’s convenient for building, tagging, and pushing OCI images, but it’s a severe threat vector in pipelines tasked with provisioning local hardware, edge gateways, or localized proxies.

[ Compromised CI/CD Runner Component ]

│

▼ (Executes malicious payload)

[ /var/run/docker.sock API Call ]

│

▼ (Escalates instantly to Host Root)

[ Physical Edge Hardware / Host OS Assets Compromised ]

When a pipeline has an explicit path to physical equipment — IIoT interfaces, SCADA networks, medical systems, or edge AI nodes — a container escape is not a localized software failure. It’s a vector for cyber-physical damage. Because dockerd runs natively as root, any process that can reach its unencrypted UNIX socket can issue arbitrary API calls: docker run --privileged -v /:/host from a compromised dependency is enough to compromise the host OS outright.

This isn’t a hypothetical. CVE-2024-21626 (“Leaky Vessels”), disclosed in January 2024, showed exactly this class of failure in practice: an internal file-descriptor leak in runc (versions up to 1.1.11) let a malicious image or runc exec payload set a container’s working directory to a leaked file descriptor pointing at the host filesystem — landing the container process outside its own root, with a documented path to overwriting host binaries. It was fixed in runc 1.1.12, but it’s a clean illustration of why the blast radius of a runtime escape matters as much as the probability of one. A daemon running as root turns any such bug into full host compromise; a properly configured rootless pipeline turns the same bug into a low-privilege service account with no path to system binaries, kernel modules, or physical devices.

To protect industrial infrastructure, modern architecture requires moving away from the monolithic container daemon entirely toward isolation mechanisms where container creation and image builds execute natively inside unprivileged user namespaces — the approach Podman and Buildah were built around.

• Technical Breakdown: Podman and Buildah Architecture
Traditional Docker architecture:

[ Client CLI ] ──(UNIX socket/TCP)──► [ Central Monolithic Daemon (dockerd) ] ──► [ OCI Runtime (runc) ]

Daemonless architecture:

[ Podman / Buildah CLI ] ──(Direct Linux fork/exec via namespaces)──► [ OCI Runtime (crun / runc) ]

Docker’s client-server model translates CLI input into REST calls sent over a socket to dockerd, a continuously running root process that manages every container as a child. If the daemon fails, orchestration for every container on the host breaks with it.

Podman and Buildah instead follow a fork-exec model close to the traditional Linux process lifecycle. The binary is an ephemeral utility: it talks directly to the kernel via an OCI-compliant runtime (crun or runc), tracks state through the decentralized containers/storage library, and exits once the task completes. There’s no long-lived background service and no exposed API socket.

Core components

Podman (Pod Manager) — manages, runs, and debugs pods and OCI containers (run, stop, ps, exec) without a daemon.

Buildah — a focused utility for constructing OCI images. Podman uses Buildah’s libraries internally for podman build; standalone Buildah exposes granular, scriptable, layer-by-layer build commands.

Skopeo — inspects, signs, and copies images across registries and local storage without pulling and unpacking full layers.

User namespaces: the mechanism behind rootless execution

Rootless containment relies on Linux user namespaces (user_namespaces(7)), which map a range of host UIDs/GIDs to a different set inside the container. When an unprivileged account starts a rootless Podman instance, the kernel maps the user’s real UID (say, 1001) to UID 0 inside the container’s namespace. Any attempt by that process to reach outside the container boundary gets translated back to UID 1001 on the host.

If a supply-chain attack lands a malicious payload in a container running via a rootless pipeline, and the payload exploits a runtime vulnerability to break out, the attacker lands on the host as that same low-privilege service account — not root. They can’t modify system binaries, load kernel modules, or touch physical devices directly.

• Configuring User Namespaces for Hardware Provisioning
Rootless namespace mapping depends on the host’s /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid files, which allocate blocks of subordinate UIDs/GIDs to unprivileged accounts.

gitlab-runner:100000:65536

Three colon-delimited fields: - Identifier — the host service account (gitlab-runner) - Initial subordinate UID — start of the allocated block (100000) - Range count — number of UIDs granted (65536)

Container-internal UID Host UID mapping Description

0 (internal root) 1001 The service account’s real host UID

1 100000 First subordinate UID in the range

2 100001 Second subordinate UID

65535 165535 Final subordinate UID in the block

Modern package managers now provision this automatically — sudo usermod --add-subuids 100000-165535 --add-subgids 100000-165535 — but pipeline service accounts created by infrastructure-as-code tooling still need it set explicitly, and it’s worth verifying with grep /etc/subuid /etc/subgid rather than assuming it exists.

Storage drivers: native overlay has mostly replaced fuse-overlayfs

The original constraint here was real but is now dated, and it’s worth being precise about what’s changed. Rootful containers use the kernel’s overlay filesystem directly. For years, unprivileged users couldn’t mount overlay at all, so rootless Podman fell back to fuse-overlayfs, a FUSE-based user-space implementation that handles copy-on-write inside the user namespace without touching the host’s privileged mount subsystem.

That workaround is no longer strictly necessary on current kernels. Linux 5.11 added unprivileged overlay mounting, but a SELinux-labeling bug meant it wasn’t safe to rely on until kernel 5.13, where the fix landed. Since then, Podman has preferred native rootless overlay whenever the kernel supports it, because fuse-overlayfs is a user-space filesystem that has to interpret every read/write before handing it to the host kernel — real, measurable overhead under heavy build or write workloads. fuse-overlayfs is still relevant as a fallback for older kernels, for NFS-backed home directories (which don’t tolerate multiple UIDs server-side), or in specific container-in-container scenarios where /dev/fuse is easier to grant than full native overlay support.

Practical implication for pipeline design: check podman info | grep -i "native overlay" (or Native Overlay Diff: true in the info output) on your runner hosts before assuming you need fuse-overlayfs at all. On a 5.13+ kernel, the recommended ~/.config/containers/storage.conf no longer needs a mount_program line:

[storage]

driver = "overlay"

[storage.options.overlay]

Native rootless overlay is used automatically on kernel 5.13+.

Only uncomment the next two lines as a fallback for older kernels,

NFS-backed home directories, or nested-container scenarios.

mount_program = "/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs"

mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on"

metacopy=on still applies either way — it avoids copying lower-layer file data until it’s actually modified, which meaningfully speeds up builds with large base images.

• Blueprint for Daemonless Image Construction with Buildah
Buildah is well suited to programmatic, scripted builds rather than static Dockerfile parsing, which is useful when a pipeline needs to pull local configuration matrices, compile firmware binaries, and assemble a hardened image without extra intermediate layers.

!/usr/bin/env bash

set -o errexit

set -o pipefail

set -o nounset

Step 1: Initialize a clean working environment from a minimal base image

echo "=== Initializing base OCI container ==="

container=$(buildah from alpine:3.19)

Step 2: Mount the container's root filesystem into the host's unprivileged space

echo "=== Exposing container filesystem layer ==="

container_mount=$(buildah mount "${container}")

Step 3: Provision the application layer

echo "=== Provisioning application layer and edge artifacts ==="

mkdir -p "${container_mount}/opt/edge/proxy"

mkdir -p "${container_mount}/etc/edge"

echo "Initializing hardened communication loop" > "${container_mount}/opt/edge/proxy/core.bin"

chmod 755 "${container_mount}/opt/edge/proxy/core.bin"

Step 4: Configure the container

echo "=== Applying container configuration ==="

buildah config --workingdir "/opt/edge/proxy" "${container}"

buildah config --entrypoint '["/opt/edge/proxy/core.bin"]' "${container}"

buildah config --user "1001:1001" "${container}"

buildah config --label architecture="edge-hardened" "${container}"

buildah config --env PROXY_PORT="8080" "${container}"

Step 5: Commit to local immutable storage

echo "=== Committing sealed OCI image ==="

buildah commit --rm "${container}" "localhost/edge-proxy:v1.0.0"

echo "=== Build complete: localhost/edge-proxy:v1.0.0 ==="

Why this matters in practice: - No exposed sockets — the whole build happens inside the pipeline process itself, with no background daemon involved. - Granular file control — buildah mount gives direct filesystem access, avoiding the layer bloat of repeated RUN cp / ADD instructions. - Non-root target definition — buildah config --user "1001:1001" guarantees the shipped image runs unprivileged on target hardware.

As of mid-2026, Buildah is at v1.44.0 (released May 27, 2026) and Podman’s stable line is at v5.8.x (v5.8.2, April 2026) — worth pinning explicitly in CI images rather than tracking :latest, since both projects ship security fixes frequently (more on that in the checklist below).

• Implementation Guide: Secure Edge Provisioning Pipeline
The following uses GitLab CI/CD syntax; the structure maps directly to GitHub Actions, Tekton, or Jenkins.

stages:

• lint

• build

• provision

variables:

# Isolate storage layers inside the local runner workspace

CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF: "$CI_PROJECT_DIR/.containers/storage.conf"

REGISTRY_AUTH_FILE: "$CI_PROJECT_DIR/.containers/auth.json"

STORAGE_DRIVER: "overlay"

default:

before_script:

- mkdir -p $(dirname "$CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF")

- |

echo '[storage]' > "$CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF"

echo 'driver = "overlay"' >> "$CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF"

# Native overlay is used automatically on kernel 5.13+;

# uncomment the two lines below only if your runner kernel predates that.

# echo '[storage.options.overlay]' >> "$CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF"

#
